Q1: I tried to set up a routine where I'd study about three to four hours each day, mostly in the evenings after my other obligations. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time with diagrams and charts, trying to make sense of the material that way. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan my study sessions around when I could access campus computers or borrow a friend's connection. It was messy, honestly, and I often felt like I was playing catch-up rather than staying ahead.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and when I could actually get online. I wanted to be more consistent, but life kept getting in the way—sometimes I'd have to help out at home or deal with other responsibilities that ate into my study time. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cram more than I'd like to admit. I'd have these bursts of intense studying followed by days where I barely touched anything.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or needing to watch a lecture video I'd missed. Since my attendance was pretty low—I know, I'm not proud of it—the LMS was my main way of keeping up with what was happening in class. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if grades had been posted. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing would push me to check in.
Q4: For a while, yeah, logging into the LMS became something I did almost automatically when I got access to a computer. I'd check the course page, scan through new materials, and queue up videos to watch. It became routine because I knew if I didn't stay on top of it, I'd fall too far behind to recover. But that routine broke down when things got overwhelming later in the semester.
Q5: The video lectures were the most helpful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. I spent a lot of time with those—over two hours sometimes in a single session. The visual materials like slides and diagrams also helped me grasp concepts better than just reading text. Discussion posts were useful too because seeing how other students understood things gave me different perspectives.
Q6: I tended to skip over the lengthy reading materials and some of the optional practice exercises. Honestly, with limited internet access, I had to prioritize what I could realistically engage with. The readings felt less efficient for my learning style, and I couldn't easily download everything to study offline. I know I probably missed important details by skipping them, but I had to make choices about where to spend my limited online time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I watched a video and immediately understood something I'd been confused about, that felt valuable. I also appreciated activities that were visual and interactive rather than just walls of text. When something helped me feel less lost in the course, that's when I knew it was worth the effort.
Q8: Required activities always had this weight of obligation attached to them—I'd do them even if I was exhausted because I knew they affected my grade. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, especially when I was struggling to keep up with the basics. Sometimes I'd look at optional resources and think 'I should do this,' but then a deadline would loom and I'd have to let them go. It made me sad because I genuinely wanted to learn more deeply.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. Since I don't have reliable internet at home, I'd download lecture slides and any PDFs I could get my hands on during my time online. Then I'd study from those materials for hours without being connected. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen, just me and my notebook.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with printed materials or downloaded slides, working through problems and making diagrams by hand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts—that peer interaction wasn't captured either. My LMS activity probably looked sporadic, but I was genuinely putting in the hours offline.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download or take detailed notes from it, and then work with that material offline for days. My internet situation forced me to be strategic like that. I'd watch a lecture video once online, but then I'd review my notes from it five or six more times at home. The LMS only saw that one access, not all the studying that followed.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. The workload piled up, my stress levels increased, and I started feeling really sad about my performance. I was attending less because I felt embarrassed about falling behind, which only made things worse. The CBT sessions I had helped my mood eventually, but during that rough patch, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. I'd look at all the materials I'd missed and just feel paralyzed.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from trying to balance everything with limited resources, I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities at home meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and helping my family. The confusion from missing classes made the LMS materials harder to follow, which made me less motivated to engage. It became this cycle where falling behind made it harder to catch up.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about my exam scores, which were really disappointing, or worrying about whether I'd pass. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement in the data, but I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed working on assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was that I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Part of it was this low motivation that made everything feel pointless—like, what's the use if I'm already doing poorly? The sadness I was feeling made it easy to avoid things that felt difficult. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have these intense periods right before something was due where I'd log in repeatedly, watch videos back-to-back, and scramble to complete work. Without a deadline, I'd often drift and not engage as much. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a due date was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and get me moving.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially if it was encouraging or showed me what I did wrong, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But sometimes the feedback came too late to help, or it was minimal and I didn't know how to improve. Negative feedback on my exam scores really hit hard and made me want to disengage. The CBT sessions helped me process that disappointment better, but it was still tough.
Q18: The course structure with all those online components actually worked okay for my situation since I couldn't attend much in person. Having 14 courses to manage was overwhelming though, and this course sometimes got pushed aside. The way materials were organized in modules helped me know what to prioritize, but the sheer volume made it hard to keep up. I appreciated having flexibility, but I also needed more structure than I gave myself.
Q19: Having video lectures available was great for my visual learning style and my attendance issues. But some aspects made engagement harder—like when materials assumed you'd been in class or when discussions moved too fast for me to contribute meaningfully. The lack of clear guidance on what was essential versus optional made me anxious about missing something important. Better signposting would have helped me use my limited online time more effectively.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard to understand but struggling with the material. They might watch videos multiple times, read discussions, search for help—all that activity—but still not be able to produce quality work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to focus, so they're present but not productive. It could also be that the assignments don't match what they're learning from the materials.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some people might be efficient learners who don't need to spend as much time in the LMS. Or they might have strong external resources—good textbooks, study groups, tutoring—that help them succeed without heavy LMS use. For me, my offline studying was significant but invisible. Someone looking at my low attendance and moderate LMS activity wouldn't see all the hours I spent with downloaded materials.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend. That's useful for seeing general engagement trends. But it completely misses the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. It can't capture whether someone is actually understanding the material or just going through motions. It also misses the emotional and psychological factors that affect how someone learns.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to learn more, when you think about course concepts outside of study time. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to the course somehow—whether through the content, the instructor, or other students. It's hard to feel engaged when you're struggling and sad, even if you're technically doing the activities.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how the CBT sessions helped me feel better toward the end. It wouldn't capture all my offline studying or the conversations with classmates that helped me understand things. It also wouldn't show how my limited internet access shaped everything I did—every login was precious and planned. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually tried hard and learned things, even if my exam scores don't reflect that.
